sentences.chinese: 卷四十九 爰盎晁錯傳第十九
sentences.literal: Scroll 49: Biography 19 of Yuan Ang and Chao Cuo.
sentences.idiomatic: Volume 49: The Biographies of Yuan Ang and Chao Cuo, number nineteen.

sentences.chinese: 絳侯為丞相，朝罷趨出，意得甚。
sentences.literal: The Marquis of Jiang was chancellor; when court ended he hurried out, very pleased with himself.
sentences.idiomatic: When the Marquis of Jiang left court, he hastened from the hall with an air of immense self-satisfaction.

sentences.chinese: 上禮之恭，常目送之。
sentences.literal: The emperor treated him with deference and often watched him depart with his eyes.
sentences.idiomatic: The Son of Heaven showed him marked courtesy and would stand watching until he was out of sight.

sentences.chinese: 盎進曰：「丞相何如人也？」
sentences.literal: Ang stepped forward and said, "What sort of man is the chancellor?"
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang stepped forward and asked, "What manner of minister is the chancellor?"

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「社稷臣。」
sentences.literal: The emperor said, "A minister of the altars of soil and grain."
sentences.idiomatic: He replied, "A pillar of the state."

sentences.chinese: 盎曰：「絳侯所謂功臣，非社稷臣。
sentences.literal: Ang said, "What the Marquis of Jiang is may be called a meritorious subject; he is not a minister of the altars of soil and grain.
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang said, "The Marquis of Jiang is a man who earned his place by merit; he is not the kind of minister whose fate is bound to the altars of state."

sentences.chinese: 社稷臣主在與在，主亡與亡。
sentences.literal: A minister of the altars is one who, when the ruler lives, lives with him; when the ruler perishes, perishes with him.
sentences.idiomatic: The true minister of the altars shares the ruler's life and shares his death—there is no separating their fortunes.

sentences.chinese: 方呂後時，諸呂用事，擅相王，劉氏不絕如帶。
sentences.literal: In the time of Empress Lü, the Lü clan held power, arrogated the right to make kings among themselves, and the house of Liu hung by a thread.
sentences.idiomatic: Under Empress Lü, her kinsmen ran the government, handed out kingdoms as they pleased, and the Liu imperial line survived by a hair's breadth.

sentences.chinese: 是時絳侯為太尉，本兵柄，弗能正。
sentences.literal: At that time the Marquis of Jiang was Grand Commandant and held the military authority, yet could not set things right.
sentences.idiomatic: The Marquis of Jiang was Grand Commandant with the army in his hands, yet he failed to correct the situation.

sentences.chinese: 呂後崩，大臣相與共誅諸呂，太尉主兵，適會其成功，所謂功臣，非社稷臣。
sentences.literal: When Empress Lü died, the great ministers together executed the Lü clan; the Grand Commandant commanded the troops and happened to coincide with their success—what is called a meritorious subject, not a minister of the altars.
sentences.idiomatic: After her death the senior ministers rose together and extirpated the Lü; the Grand Commandant led the soldiery and was there when victory came—that is a man who seized his moment of merit, not a minister whose loyalty is welded to the dynasty itself.

sentences.chinese: 丞相如有驕主色，陛下謙讓，臣主失禮，竊為陛下弗取也。」
sentences.literal: If the chancellor shows a look of arrogance toward the ruler, while Your Majesty yields to him, ruler and subject lose propriety; I privately think Your Majesty should not accept this."
sentences.idiomatic: If the chancellor wears insolence toward his sovereign while you defer to him, the proper order between lord and minister is broken; I beg you not to indulge it."

sentences.chinese: 後朝，上益莊，丞相益畏。
sentences.literal: Afterward at court the emperor grew more solemn and the chancellor more fearful.
sentences.idiomatic: At the next audiences the emperor carried himself with greater gravity, and the chancellor grew visibly uneasy.

sentences.chinese: 已而絳侯望盎曰：「吾與汝兄善，今兒乃毀我！」
sentences.literal: Presently the Marquis of Jiang glared at Ang and said, "I was on good terms with your elder brother; now you, the boy, actually slander me!"
sentences.idiomatic: Soon the Marquis of Jiang fixed Yuan Ang with a stare and said, "Your brother and I were friends—how dare you, his junior, speak ill of me?"

sentences.chinese: 盎遂不謝。
sentences.literal: Ang then did not apologize.
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang offered no apology.

sentences.chinese: 及絳侯就國，人上書告以為反，征系請室，諸公莫敢為言，唯盎明絳侯無罪。
sentences.literal: When the Marquis of Jiang went to his fief, someone submitted a memorial accusing him of treason; he was summoned, bound, and held in the Qingshi prison, and none of the nobles dared speak for him—only Ang declared the Marquis of Jiang innocent.
sentences.idiomatic: After the Marquis of Jiang retired to his state, a denunciation reached the throne accusing him of rebellion; he was arrested and locked in the Qingshi interrogation cell, and not one of the great lords would plead his case—only Yuan Ang swore he was guiltless.

sentences.chinese: 絳侯得釋，盎頗有力。
sentences.literal: The Marquis of Jiang was released, and Ang had considerable part in it.
sentences.idiomatic: The marquis was freed, and Yuan Ang's intervention counted for much.

sentences.chinese: 絳侯乃大與盎結交。
sentences.literal: The Marquis of Jiang then formed a deep friendship with Ang.
sentences.idiomatic: From that day the Marquis of Jiang counted Yuan Ang among his closest allies.

sentences.chinese: 淮南厲王朝，殺辟陽侯，居處驕甚。
sentences.literal: The Prince Li of Huainan attended court, killed the Marquis of Piyang, and in his conduct and lodging was extremely arrogant.
sentences.idiomatic: Prince Li of Huainan came to the capital, slew Marquis of Piyang, and carried himself with outrageous arrogance.

sentences.chinese: 盎諫曰：「諸侯太驕必生患，可適削地。」
sentences.literal: Ang remonstrated, saying, "If the feudal lords grow too arrogant disaster will surely follow; one may fittingly pare back their lands."
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang urged the throne: "Overmighty vassals breed calamity; Your Majesty should trim their domains while there is still time."

sentences.chinese: 上弗許。
sentences.literal: The emperor did not grant permission.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor refused.

sentences.chinese: 淮南王益橫。
sentences.literal: The King of Huainan grew more overbearing.
sentences.idiomatic: The king of Huainan only grew bolder.

sentences.chinese: 謀反發覺，上征淮南王，遷之蜀，檻車傳送。
sentences.literal: When his plot to rebel was discovered, the emperor summoned the King of Huainan, banished him to Shu, and sent him under escort in a caged cart.
sentences.idiomatic: When his treason came to light, the emperor recalled him, reassigned him to the Shu frontier, and shipped him west in a prison wagon.

sentences.chinese: 盎時為中郎將，諫曰：「陛下素驕之，弗稍禁，以至此，今又暴摧折之。
sentences.literal: Ang was then General of the Gentlemen of the Household and remonstrated, saying, "Your Majesty has always indulged him and never checked him in the least, so that matters reached this pass; now you would suddenly crush him.
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang, then a chief of the household guard, said: "You have spoiled him for years without a word of restraint until things came to a head; now you mean to break him in an instant."

sentences.chinese: 淮南王為人剛，有如遇霜露行道死，陛下竟為以天下大弗能容，有殺弟名，奈何？」
sentences.literal: The King of Huainan is by nature stubborn; if perchance he dies of cold or exposure on the road, in the end the realm will say Your Majesty could not tolerate even him, and you will bear the name of one who slew a younger brother—what then?"
sentences.idiomatic: He is a proud man; if he should sicken and die on the march, the empire will say you could not abide your own brother and you will wear the stain of fratricide—how will you answer that?"

sentences.chinese: 上不聽，遂行之。
sentences.literal: The emperor did not listen and proceeded with it.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor would not be moved and carried out the transfer.

sentences.chinese: 淮南王至雍，病死。
sentences.literal: When the King of Huainan reached Yong, he died of illness.
sentences.idiomatic: By the time he reached Yong he was dead—officially, of illness.

sentences.chinese: 聞，上輟食，哭甚哀。
sentences.literal: When the emperor heard it, he stopped eating and wept very bitterly.
sentences.idiomatic: Word reached the throne; the emperor pushed away his meal and wept as if his heart would break.

sentences.chinese: 盎入，頓首請罪。
sentences.literal: Ang entered, knocked his head to the ground, and asked to be punished.
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang came in, kowtowed, and asked to be punished for his bad counsel.

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「以不用公言至此。」
sentences.literal: The emperor said, "Because I did not use your words, it has come to this."
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor said, "We are here because I would not heed you."

sentences.chinese: 盎曰：「上自寬，此往事，豈可悔哉！
sentences.literal: Ang said, "Your Majesty should console yourself; this is past—how can it be regretted!
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang said, "Set your mind at ease, Son of Heaven—what is done cannot be undone."

sentences.chinese: 且陛下有高世行三，此不足以毀名。」
sentences.literal: Moreover Your Majesty has three deeds that tower above the age; this is not enough to ruin your name."
sentences.idiomatic: Besides, you have three acts that set you above ordinary rulers; this affair cannot undo your reputation."

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「吾高世三者何事？」
sentences.literal: The emperor said, "My three deeds that tower above the age—what are they?"
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor asked what those three were.

sentences.chinese: 盎曰：「陛下居代時，太后嘗病，三年，陛下不交睫解衣，湯藥非陛下口所嘗弗進。
sentences.literal: When Your Majesty dwelt in Dai, the empress dowager once fell ill for three years; Your Majesty did not close your eyes or remove your garments, and no decoction passed her lips unless you had tasted it first.
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang said: "In your years at Dai the empress dowager lay ill three full years; you went without sleep and without changing your robes, and no draught touched her unless you had sipped it first."

sentences.chinese: 夫曾參以布衣猶難之，今陛下親以王者修之，過曾參遠矣。
sentences.literal: Even Zeng Shen in plain cloth still found that hard; now Your Majesty, in the person of a king, practiced it—you surpass Zeng Shen by far.
sentences.idiomatic: Even Zeng Shen, a commoner, struggled to match such devotion; you did it as heir to a kingdom—you have outdone Zeng Shen by a wide margin.

sentences.chinese: 諸呂用事，大臣顓制，然陛下從代乘六乘傳，馳不測淵，雖賁、育之勇不及陛下。
sentences.literal: When the Lü held power and great ministers monopolized control, yet Your Majesty came from Dai riding six relay teams, galloping into unfathomable peril—even the courage of Ben and Yu did not match Your Majesty.
sentences.idiomatic: When the Lü faction held the court in thrall and great ministers ruled by fiat, you rode post-haste from Dai with a six-horse relay train into mortal uncertainty—courage like Ben and Yu's would not have matched yours.

sentences.chinese: 陛下至代邸，西鄉讓天子者三，南鄉讓天子者再。
sentences.literal: When Your Majesty reached the Dai lodge, you thrice yielded the Son of Heaven facing west and twice yielded facing south.
sentences.idiomatic: At the lodge in Dai you declined the imperial seat three times while facing west, and twice more while facing south.

sentences.chinese: 夫許由一讓，陛下五以天下讓，過許由四矣。
sentences.literal: Xu You yielded once; Your Majesty yielded the empire five times—you surpass Xu You by four.
sentences.idiomatic: Xu You yielded the world once; you yielded it five times—you have exceeded him fourfold.

sentences.chinese: 且陛下遷淮南王，欲以苦其志，使改過，有司宿衛不謹，故病死。」
sentences.literal: Moreover, in banishing the King of Huainan, Your Majesty wished to temper his will and make him reform his faults; the officials on night watch were not careful, and therefore he died of illness."
sentences.idiomatic: You sent him away to humble his spirit and teach him amendment; the guards were negligent and he died—that is the truth of it, not your hand."

sentences.chinese: 於是上乃解，盎繇此名重朝廷。
sentences.literal: Thereupon the emperor's mind was eased, and from this Ang's name weighed heavy at court.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor's grief lifted, and Yuan Ang's standing in the capital was never higher.

sentences.chinese: 盎常引大體慷慨。
sentences.literal: Ang often cited the great principles and spoke with fervor.
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang was known for invoking the larger interests of the state in passionate terms.

sentences.chinese: 宦者趙談以數幸，常害盎，盎患之。
sentences.literal: The eunuch Zhao Tan, because he was repeatedly favored, constantly harmed Ang, and Ang worried about it.
sentences.idiomatic: The palace eunuch Zhao Tan, often in the emperor's confidence, worked steadily against him, and Yuan Ang lived in dread of it.

sentences.chinese: 盎兄子種為常侍騎，諫盎曰：「君眾辱之，後雖惡君，上不覆信。」
sentences.literal: Ang's nephew Zhong, who was a mounted attendant of the household, remonstrated with Ang, saying, "If you publicly humiliate him, afterward though he hates you, the emperor will no longer wholly trust him."
sentences.idiomatic: His nephew Zhong, a household cavalry attendant, warned him: "Humiliate him before the court and, however much he hates you afterward, the Son of Heaven will not give him the same credence."

sentences.chinese: 於是上朝東宮，趙談驂乘，盎伏車前曰：「臣聞天子所與共六尺輿者，皆天下豪英。
sentences.literal: Thereupon at court in the Eastern Palace Zhao Tan rode as attendant in the chariot; Ang prostrated himself before the chariot and said, "I have heard that those who share the six-foot carriage with the Son of Heaven are all the heroes and worthies of the realm.
sentences.idiomatic: At the next audience in the Eastern Palace Zhao Tan took the attendant's seat in the imperial car; Yuan Ang threw himself across the shafts and said, "They say whoever rides beside the ruler must be a champion of the empire."

sentences.chinese: 今漢雖乏人，陛下獨奈何與刀鋸之余共載！」
sentences.literal: Now Han may lack men, but how can Your Majesty alone share a carriage with the leavings of knife and saw!"
sentences.idiomatic: Han may be short of talent, but must you share your coach with a man mutilated by the executioner's blade?"

sentences.chinese: 於是上笑，下趙談。
sentences.literal: Thereupon the emperor laughed and dismissed Zhao Tan from the chariot.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor laughed and ordered Zhao Tan down from the chariot.

sentences.chinese: 談泣下車。
sentences.literal: Tan wept as he left the chariot.
sentences.idiomatic: Zhao Tan climbed down in tears.

sentences.chinese: 上從霸陵上，欲西馳下峻阪，盎攬轡。
sentences.literal: When the emperor went up on the Baling tumulus and wished to gallop west down a steep slope, Ang seized the reins.
sentences.idiomatic: On a visit to Baling the emperor wished to race his team down the western escarpment; Yuan Ang caught the horses' bits.

sentences.chinese: 上曰：「將軍怯邪？」
sentences.literal: The emperor said, "Is the general a coward?"
sentences.idiomatic: "Are you afraid?" asked the emperor.

sentences.chinese: 盎言曰：「臣聞千金之子不垂堂，百金之子不騎衡，聖主不乘危，不僥倖。
sentences.literal: Ang said, "I have heard that the son of a thousand in gold does not sit beneath a high hall, the son of a hundred in gold does not straddle the crossbar of a scale, and the enlightened ruler does not take risks or trust to luck.
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang replied: "They say the heir to a great fortune does not court danger under a high roof, the heir to a middling fortune does not ride the balance-beam, and a wise ruler neither hazards his person nor trusts to chance."

sentences.chinese: 今陛下聘六飛，馳不測山，有如馬驚車敗，陛下縱自輕，奈高廟、太后何？」
sentences.literal: Now Your Majesty drives six flying horses, galloping over unfathomable hills; if the horses should bolt and the chariot overturn, though Your Majesty might think lightly of yourself, what of Gaozu's temple and the empress dowager?"
sentences.idiomatic: Yet here you urge six thoroughbreds over a sheer mountainside—if a wheel slips or a horse shies, you may think your own life cheap, but what of the shrines of Gaozu and the empress dowager?"

sentences.chinese: 上乃止。
sentences.literal: The emperor then stopped.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor reined in.

sentences.chinese: 上幸上林，皇后、慎夫人從。
sentences.literal: The emperor visited Shanglin, and the empress and Lady Shen accompanied him.
sentences.idiomatic: When the Son of Heaven hunted Shanglin, the empress and Lady Shen went along.

sentences.chinese: 其在禁中，常同坐。
sentences.literal: When they were inside the forbidden quarters, they often sat together.
sentences.idiomatic: Inside the inner palace they were often given seats side by side.

sentences.chinese: 及坐，郎署長布席，盎引卻慎夫人坐。
sentences.literal: When it came time to sit, the chief of the gentleman attendants spread the mats, and Ang drew back Lady Shen's seat.
sentences.idiomatic: At seating time the chief usher arranged the cushions; Yuan Ang moved Lady Shen's place to the rear.

sentences.chinese: 慎夫人怒，不肯坐。
sentences.literal: Lady Shen was angry and refused to sit.
sentences.idiomatic: Lady Shen flared up and would not take her seat.

sentences.chinese: 上亦起，起。
sentences.literal: The emperor also rose and rose again.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor got to his feet as well and remained standing, ill at ease.

sentences.chinese: 盎因前說曰：「臣聞尊卑有序則上下和，今陛下既以立後，慎夫人乃妾，妾、主豈可以同坐哉！
sentences.literal: Ang therefore stepped forward and said, "I have heard that when high and low keep their order, above and below are harmonious; now Your Majesty has already established an empress, while Lady Shen is a concubine—how can concubine and mistress share the same seat!
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang said: "Rank exists for a reason; you have named a queen, and Lady Shen is a concubine—lord and servant cannot share one bench."

sentences.chinese: 且陛下幸之，則厚賜之。
sentences.literal: Moreover when Your Majesty favors her, then richly reward her.
sentences.idiomatic: Honor her with gifts and titles if you favor her.

sentences.chinese: 陛下所以為慎夫人，適所以禍之也。
sentences.literal: What Your Majesty does for Lady Shen is precisely what will bring disaster upon her.
sentences.idiomatic: The favor you think you show her is the very thing that will destroy her.

sentences.chinese: 獨不見『人豕』乎？」
sentences.literal: Have you alone not seen the 'human pig'?"
sentences.idiomatic: Have you forgotten the fate of the 'human swine'?"

sentences.chinese: 於是上乃說，入語慎夫人。
sentences.literal: Thereupon the emperor was pleased, went in, and spoke to Lady Shen.
sentences.idiomatic: The emperor saw his point, went within, and explained it to Lady Shen.

sentences.chinese: 慎夫人賜盎金五十斤。
sentences.literal: Lady Shen gave Ang fifty catties of gold.
sentences.idiomatic: Lady Shen sent him fifty catties of gold in thanks.

sentences.chinese: 然盎亦以數直諫，不得久居中。
sentences.literal: Yet Ang also because he repeatedly remonstrated bluntly could not long remain at the center.
sentences.idiomatic: Because he spoke his mind too often, he could not long stay at court.

sentences.chinese: 調為隴西都尉，仁愛士卒，士卒皆爭為死。
sentences.literal: He was transferred to be commandant of Longxi; he loved the soldiers benevolently, and the soldiers all vied to die for him.
sentences.idiomatic: Posted as commandant of Longxi, he treated his men with kindness and they would have died for him willingly.

sentences.chinese: 遷齊相，徒為吳相。
sentences.literal: He was promoted to chancellor of Qi, then transferred to be chancellor of Wu.
sentences.idiomatic: He rose to chancellor of Qi, then was moved to the same office in Wu.

sentences.chinese: 辭行，種謂盎曰：「吳王驕日久，國多奸，今絲欲刻治，彼不上書告君，則利劍刺君矣。
sentences.literal: On his departure Zhong said to Ang, "The King of Wu has long been arrogant; the state swarms with scoundrels; now if you, Si, wish to govern strictly, he will either submit a memorial accusing you or run you through with a sharp sword.
sentences.idiomatic: As he set out, Zhong warned him: "The king of Wu has been proud a long time; his domain crawls with villains. If you try to rule him with a tight rein, he will denounce you to the throne or put a blade in your ribs."

sentences.chinese: 南方卑濕，絲能日飲，亡何，說王毋反而已。
sentences.literal: The south is low and damp; Si can drink daily, do nothing else, and merely persuade the king not to rebel.
sentences.idiomatic: The south is steamy and low; drink your wine every day, mind your own business, and do no more than talk him out of rebellion.

sentences.chinese: 如此幸得脫。」
sentences.literal: In this way you may perhaps escape."
sentences.idiomatic: That is how you may come through alive."

sentences.chinese: 盎用種之計，吳王厚遇盎。
sentences.literal: Ang employed Zhong's plan, and the King of Wu treated Ang generously.
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang followed the advice, and the king of Wu showered him with hospitality.

sentences.chinese: 盎告歸，道逢丞相申屠嘉，下車拜謁，丞相從車上謝。
sentences.literal: When Ang took leave to return home, on the road he met Chancellor Shen Tu Jia, dismounted, and bowed in obeisance; the chancellor from his chariot returned thanks.
sentences.idiomatic: On leave bound for home he met Chancellor Shen Tu Jia, dismounted to pay his respects, and received only a nod from the passing carriage.

sentences.chinese: 盎還，愧其吏，乃之丞相捨上謁，求見丞相。
sentences.literal: Ang returned, felt ashamed before his clerks, and therefore went to the chancellor's lodging to pay a formal visit and request an audience.
sentences.idiomatic: Mortified before his own staff, he went to the chancellor's gate and asked for a formal interview.

sentences.chinese: 丞相良久乃見。
sentences.literal: The chancellor only after a long while received him.
sentences.idiomatic: He was kept waiting a long time before being admitted.

sentences.chinese: 因跪曰：「願請間。」
sentences.literal: He therefore knelt and said, "I beg to speak in private."
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang knelt and asked for a private word.

sentences.chinese: 丞相曰：「使君所言公事，之曹與長史掾議之，吾且奏之；
sentences.literal: The chancellor said, "If what the lord says is public business, take it to the bureau and discuss it with the chief clerk and his assistants, and I will memorialize it;
sentences.idiomatic: The chancellor said: "If this is state business, take it to my office and let the senior clerks hear it; I will memorialize whatever they endorse;"

sentences.chinese: 則私，吾不受私語。」
sentences.literal: if it is private, I do not accept private words."
sentences.idiomatic: if it is personal, I do not hear private pleas."

sentences.chinese: 盎即起說曰：「君為相，自度孰與陳平、絳侯？」
sentences.literal: Ang immediately rose and said, "You are chancellor—do you measure yourself against Chen Ping and the Marquis of Jiang?"
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang rose and said: "You sit as chancellor—how do you rank yourself beside Chen Ping and the Marquis of Jiang?"

sentences.chinese: 丞相曰：「不如。」
sentences.literal: The chancellor said, "I am not their match."
sentences.idiomatic: "I am not in their class," said the chancellor.

sentences.chinese: 盎曰：「善，君自謂弗如。
sentences.literal: Ang said, "Good—you yourself say you are not their match.
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang said, "You admit you fall short—that is honest."

sentences.chinese: 夫陳平、絳侯輔翼高帝，定天下，為將相，而誅諸呂，存劉氏；
sentences.literal: Chen Ping and the Marquis of Jiang aided and supported Gaodi, settled the realm, became general and chancellor, executed the Lü clan, and preserved the house of Liu;
sentences.idiomatic: Chen Ping and the Marquis of Jiang raised Gaozu, won the empire, served as general and chancellor, extirpated the Lü, and saved the Liu line;"

sentences.chinese: 君乃為材官蹶張，遷為隊帥，積功至淮陽守，非有奇計攻城野戰之功。
sentences.literal: you were a crossbowman of the materials corps, rose to be a company commander, and accumulated merit until you became governor of Huaiyang—you had no marvelous stratagems or merit from storming cities or fighting in the open field.
sentences.idiomatic: you began as a crossbow-puller in the engineers, made company captain, and climbed to governor of Huaiyang on routine service—you never took a city by stratagem or won a field battle."

sentences.chinese: 且陛下從代來，每朝，郎官者上書疏，未嘗不止輦受。
sentences.literal: Moreover, since Your Majesty came from Dai, at each court when gentlemen of the palace submitted memorials, he never failed to halt the carriage and receive them.
sentences.idiomatic: Since the emperor returned from Dai, not once has he let a coach roll past a memorial from the gentlemen attendants without stopping to take it in hand.

sentences.chinese: 其言不可用，置之；
sentences.literal: If their words could not be used, he set them aside;
sentences.idiomatic: What could not be used he set aside;"

sentences.chinese: 言可采，未嘗不稱善。
sentences.literal: if the words could be adopted, he never failed to praise them well.
sentences.idiomatic: what was sound he praised without stint."

sentences.chinese: 何也？
sentences.literal: Why was this?
sentences.idiomatic: Why?"

sentences.chinese: 欲以致天下賢英士大夫，日聞所不聞，以益聖。
sentences.literal: He wished thereby to draw to him the worthy knights and great officers of the realm, daily to hear what he had not heard, and thereby to increase his sageliness.
sentences.idiomatic: To draw the best men of the empire to his ear, to learn something new every day, and to grow in wisdom.

sentences.chinese: 而君自閉箝天下之口，而日益愚。
sentences.literal: Yet you yourself shut and bolt the mouths of the realm and daily grow more foolish.
sentences.idiomatic: You have bolted every mouth in the land and grown more ignorant by the day.

sentences.chinese: 夫以聖主責愚相，君受禍不久矣。」
sentences.literal: When a sagely ruler calls a foolish chancellor to account, you will not long escape disaster."
sentences.idiomatic: When a wise sovereign finds his chief minister a fool, retribution follows soon enough."

sentences.chinese: 丞相乃再拜曰：「嘉鄙人，乃不知，將軍幸教。」
sentences.literal: The chancellor then bowed twice and said, "Jia is a rustic; I did not know; the general has favored me with instruction."
sentences.idiomatic: Shen Tu Jia bowed twice and said, "I am a boor; I did not understand—thank you for the lesson, general."

sentences.chinese: 引與入坐，為上客。
sentences.literal: He led him in to sit and treated him as a guest of honor.
sentences.idiomatic: He led Yuan Ang inside, seated him as an honored guest, and heard him out.

sentences.chinese: 及晁錯已誅，盎以泰常使吳。
sentences.literal: When Chao Cuo had already been executed, Ang was sent to Wu as Grand Master of Ceremonies.
sentences.idiomatic: After Chao Cuo's death Yuan Ang was dispatched to Wu as Grand Master of Ceremonies.

sentences.chinese: 吳王欲使將，不肯。
sentences.literal: The King of Wu wished to make him a general; he refused.
sentences.idiomatic: The king of Wu offered him a command; he declined.

sentences.chinese: 欲殺之，使一都尉以五百人圍守盎軍中。
sentences.literal: Wishing to kill him, he had a commandant surround Yuan Ang's camp with five hundred men.
sentences.idiomatic: Intent on killing him, the king detailed a commandant to pen Yuan Ang in with five hundred soldiers.

sentences.chinese: 初，盎為吳相時，從史盜私盎侍兒。
sentences.literal: Earlier, when Ang was chancellor of Wu, a clerk had secretly taken Ang's favorite concubine.
sentences.idiomatic: Years before, while Yuan Ang was chancellor in Wu, a clerk had stolen his favorite concubine.

sentences.chinese: 盎知之，弗洩，遇之如故。
sentences.literal: Ang knew it but did not reveal it and treated him as before.
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang knew but said nothing and treated the man as always.

sentences.chinese: 人有告從史，「君知女與侍者通」，乃亡去。
sentences.literal: Someone told the clerk, "The lord knows that you have been intimate with the attendant," and thereupon he fled.
sentences.idiomatic: When someone warned the clerk, "He knows about you and the girl," the man ran away.

sentences.chinese: 盎驅自追之，遂以侍者賜之，復為從史。
sentences.literal: Ang himself drove in pursuit, overtook him, gave him the concubine as a gift, and restored him as clerk.
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang chased him down in his own carriage, gave him the girl outright, and kept him on staff.

sentences.chinese: 及盎使吳見守，從史適在守盎校為司馬，乃悉以其裝繼買二石醇醪，會天寒，士卒飢渴，飲醉西南陬卒，卒皆臥。
sentences.literal: When Ang was envoy to Wu and under guard, the clerk happened to be serving as a marshal in the unit guarding Ang; he then used all his funds to buy two piculs of strong ale; it happened to be cold weather and the soldiers were hungry and thirsty; they drank until drunk in the southwest corner of the camp, and the soldiers all lay down.
sentences.idiomatic: When Yuan Ang was hemmed in at Wu, that same clerk was deputy marshal of his guard; he spent his savings on two hundred liters of spirits, and on a bitter day when the men were parched and starving he feasted the southwest watch until they collapsed drunk.

sentences.chinese: 司馬夜引盎起，曰：「君可以去矣，吳王期旦日斬君。」
sentences.literal: The marshal by night roused Ang and said, "The lord may go now; the King of Wu has fixed tomorrow morning to behead you."
sentences.idiomatic: At night the marshal shook him awake: "Leave now—the king means to take your head at dawn."

sentences.chinese: 盎弗信，曰：「何為者？」
sentences.literal: Ang did not believe it and said, "What is this about?"
sentences.idiomatic: Yuan Ang did not credit it: "What are you saying?"

sentences.chinese: 司馬曰：「臣故為君從史盜侍兒者也。」
sentences.literal: The marshal said, "I am the one who was formerly your clerk and stole the concubine."
sentences.idiomatic: "I am the clerk who stole your concubine long ago," he replied.
